heuristics.rule: Never name a number or give a yes/no on the first offer call
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter presents an offer and expects a response on the call
heuristics.action: Ask clarifying questions about level, vesting, bonus target, and equity cliff, then request a few days to consider before reconnecting
heuristics.because: You lose negotiating leverage the moment you accept or anchor a number; delay preserves all options and signals deliberateness

heuristics.rule: Value early-stage startup equity at $0 for financial planning purposes
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a compensation package from a Seed or Series A company
heuristics.action: Base all financial modeling on cash salary only and treat equity as a lottery ticket
heuristics.because: 80-90% of startups fail and liquidity may never materialize; planning around equity claims leads to real financial miscalculation

heuristics.rule: Move to signing bonus or equity refresh when base salary is capped
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter says the base salary cannot be increased further
heuristics.action: Explicitly ask whether signing bonus or equity refresh is possible, framing these as alternatives to base
heuristics.because: Signing bonuses don't affect recurring payroll budgets and equity refreshes can often be approved outside normal comp bands, making them easier for managers to approve

heuristics.rule: Use the final commitment close only once and only when genuinely ready to sign
heuristics.condition: When you have reached your target total comp number and are ready to commit
heuristics.action: Say 'If you can get to [X total comp], I'll sign today' as a binding commitment, not a negotiating probe
heuristics.because: This technique only works if it is credible; using it prematurely or insincerely destroys trust and eliminates its leverage effect

heuristics.rule: Anchor counter-offers to objective third-party data rather than personal need
heuristics.condition: When forced to name a target compensation figure during negotiation
heuristics.action: Reference levels.fyi percentile data for the specific level and role at comparable companies as the basis for your range
heuristics.because: Anchoring to market data depersonalizes the ask and makes it harder for recruiters to dismiss; it frames your number as industry-standard rather than arbitrary

heuristics.rule: Start processes with slow-moving large companies first, layer in fast-moving startups later
heuristics.condition: When running multiple interview processes simultaneously to generate competing offers
heuristics.action: Initiate Google, Amazon, Microsoft processes first (3-6 weeks), then start startup processes (1-2 weeks) so timelines converge
heuristics.because: Simultaneous offers maximize negotiation leverage; misaligned timelines force you to accept or decline before you have competitive data

heuristics.rule: Always ask for the offer level, not just the total comp number
heuristics.condition: When evaluating an offer from a company with leveled compensation bands
heuristics.action: Confirm which band level the offer is placed at and whether it is at the low, mid, or high end of that band
heuristics.because: A low end of a senior band beats a high end of a mid band for long-term career progression and future earning potential

heuristics.rule: Negotiate over phone or video, not email or text alone
heuristics.condition: When preparing to counter an offer
heuristics.action: Request a call rather than sending a written counter-offer
heuristics.because: Calls allow the recruiter to become an advocate who can 'fight for you' internally; text-only negotiation reduces the recruiter to a passive message relay

decision_factors.factor: Company stage and equity liquidity timeline
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Equity is 30-60% of total comp at large tech companies but may be worth $0 at early-stage companies; misvaluing this reverses offer rankings
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask for the 409A valuation, preferred share liquidation preferences, and estimated IPO or liquidity timeline; apply a discount rate based on stage (public=80-90%, Series C-D=60-90%, Seed/A=0%)

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A 90-day exercise window means you must pay to exercise vested options within 90 days of leaving or forfeit them; a 10-year window gives you flexibility to wait for a liquidity event
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask directly: 'How long after leaving do I have to exercise my options?'; standard is 90 days, best-in-class is 5-10 years

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A 4-year cliff (all equity vests at year 4) versus quarterly vesting after a 1-year cliff is a massive practical difference in when you can access the value
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask explicitly for the vesting schedule; confirm whether there is a cliff, the cliff duration, and the post-cliff cadence (monthly vs. quarterly)

decision_factors.factor: Presence and quality of a competing offer
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A competing offer from a company you would genuinely work for is the single most reliable lever for increasing any offer
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether you have an offer from a credible competitor at a comparable or higher total comp; never fabricate; evaluate your genuine willingness to accept it

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Two offers at the same base can differ by $100,000+ annually once equity, bonus, and signing bonus are included; comparing by base produces wrong rankings
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Build a full annual comp model: annualized equity (grant divided by vesting years), target bonus percentage, and base salary; treat signing bonus separately as non-recurring

decision_factors.factor: Signing bonus recurrence and clawback terms
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Signing bonuses are one-time; modeling them as ongoing comp inflates perceived annual earnings; clawback provisions can require repayment if you leave early
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether the signing bonus is subject to clawback and over what period; model annual comp without it

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The percentage of the company your equity represents matters more than the raw number of shares; future dilution from funding rounds affects actual value
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask: 'What percentage of the company does this grant represent on a fully diluted basis?'; compare against 409A valuation to understand dollar value

decision_factors.factor: Negotiation style: competitive firmness combined with collaborative warmth
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Research cited shows best negotiators combine firm outcome orientation with warm interpersonal approach; either alone is less effective
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your counter-offer framing expresses genuine enthusiasm for the role while being specific and firm about the compensation target

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a high signing bonus as part of the offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The signing bonus inflates the perceived total comp, masking a below-market annual salary; once you model year 2 comp without it, the offer may be substantially weaker than alternatives
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career candidates who are cash-constrained and anchor emotionally on the large one-time number

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking a startup offer with significant equity upside
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Options with a 90-day exercise window create a golden handcuff: leaving the company means either paying out-of-pocket to exercise vested options (often tens of thousands of dollars) or forfeiting them entirely
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with limited liquid savings who cannot afford to exercise options when leaving, effectively trapping them regardless of job satisfaction

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Negotiating via email instead of phone to feel less confrontational
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The recruiter cannot advocate internally on your behalf in real time; email reduces them to a passive relay and removes the social dynamic that makes recruiters want to 'fight for you'
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Introverted or conflict-averse candidates who default to written communication as a comfort mechanism

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Reneging on an accepted offer after signing
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: While legal consequences are minimal, reputation damage within the industry is real and persistent, particularly in small or tight-knit sectors where recruiters and hiring managers move between companies
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates in niche technical domains or geographic markets where professional networks are dense and interconnected

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using levels.fyi data without specifying level in the negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Being vague about comp targets while citing market data allows the recruiter to assume a lower level benchmark; specificity about level is required for the data to support your ask
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who research market rates but fail to confirm which level their offer corresponds to

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Prioritizing base salary in negotiation when base is capped
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Spending political capital pushing against a fixed base leaves signing bonus and equity refresh untouched; these alternative levers are often approvable without escalation but are never offered proactively
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: First-time negotiators who treat base salary as the only meaningful dimension of compensation

failure_patterns.pattern: Comparing offers by base salary only
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Base salary is the most visible and legible number; equity and bonus require calculation and ask-specific questions to quantify, so candidates default to the easily comparable figure
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidates accept offers that are $50,000-$100,000+ lower in actual annual compensation because the base salary appeared competitive
warning_signs: Saying 'both offers are about the same' without calculating annualized equity
Not knowing the bonus target percentage of either offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting startup equity valuations from recruiters at face value
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Recruiters present equity with optimistic future valuations that feel compelling; candidates lack the framework to apply stage-appropriate discounts
failure_patterns.consequence: Financial planning built on equity that never materializes, leading to compensation that is effectively just the base salary with no upside realized
warning_signs: Recruiter describes equity value using projected post-IPO or next-round valuations
Candidate cannot answer what the current 409A valuation is

failure_patterns.pattern: Giving a number or accepting verbally on the first offer call
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates feel social pressure to respond positively in the moment; excitement or relief at receiving an offer overrides strategic patience
failure_patterns.consequence: Forfeits negotiating leverage before any counter has been made; the recruiter registers the candidate as satisfied at the initial number
warning_signs: Saying 'that sounds great' or 'I think I can work with that' on the first call
Not knowing what questions to ask about the offer components in the moment

failure_patterns.pattern: Fabricating or exaggerating a competing offer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates know competing offers are powerful leverage but don't have one; they inflate a real offer or invent one to gain negotiating power
failure_patterns.consequence: If the recruiter asks for documentation or the fabrication is discovered, trust is destroyed and the offer may be rescinded; the tactic is explicitly flagged as something never to do
warning_signs: Citing a competing offer from a company whose process hasn't produced a written offer
Being unable to provide specifics when the recruiter asks follow-up questions about the competing offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Using Glassdoor or PayScale for tech compensation research
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: These platforms are widely known brand names for salary data; candidates don't realize they systematically mishandle equity and underreport tech compensation
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidates anchor their negotiation targets to artificially low benchmarks, leaving significant compensation on the table
warning_signs: Citing a salary range from Glassdoor that is 20-30% below levels.fyi data for the same role
Not knowing whether the cited salary figure includes equity

failure_patterns.pattern: Using the final commitment close ('I'll sign today if you reach X') too early or insincerely
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The tactic sounds powerful and candidates deploy it as a negotiating probe rather than a genuine commitment
failure_patterns.consequence: Destroys credibility with the recruiter; if the number is met and the candidate still doesn't sign, the recruiter loses trust and future negotiations are compromised
warning_signs: Making the commitment close before having fully evaluated the offer
Using the phrase more than once in the same negotiation

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you calculated the full annual total compensation for each offer, including annualized equity, target bonus, and base — excluding the signing bonus?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is comparing offers on a valid apples-to-apples basis or defaulting to base salary comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is the annualized equity value and what discount rate did you apply based on company stage?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The offer ranking may be reversed once full comp is modeled; what is the equity grant, vesting schedule, and bonus target for each offer?

diagnostic_questions.question: What stage is the company — public, pre-IPO Series C/D, or early-stage Seed/A?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Which equity valuation framework applies and how much weight to give the equity component in financial planning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: For pre-IPO: have you asked for the 409A valuation and the liquidation preference structure?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If company stage is unknown, the candidate cannot accurately value the equity or assess liquidity risk

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have a competing offer from a company you would genuinely consider working for?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the most powerful negotiation lever is available and can be used credibly
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you mentioned it to the recruiter, and have you framed your target company as the first choice?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are there other processes in flight that could produce a competing offer before the current deadline?

diagnostic_questions.question: What level is the offer, and have you confirmed whether it is at the low, mid, or high end of that band?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is evaluating near-term comp or long-term career trajectory; a misleveled offer has compounding consequences
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Does the offered level match the scope and expectations described in the role?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The candidate cannot accurately benchmark the offer or assess career progression potential without this information

diagnostic_questions.question: For option grants specifically: what is the exercise window after leaving the company?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the equity creates a golden handcuff that limits the candidate's future job mobility
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If 90 days: can you afford to exercise vested options out of pocket if you leave before a liquidity event?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Candidate may be agreeing to equity terms that trap them financially without realizing it

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you checked levels.fyi for the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentile total comp for your specific level and role at this company?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has objective market data to anchor their counter-offer or is negotiating without a benchmark
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Where does the current offer fall in that range, and what percentile are you targeting?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Candidate is likely to either anchor too low or make an ask that is not credibly supported by data

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you genuinely willing to walk away from this offer if the company does not meet your target number?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the negotiation is real or performative; the source states that the final close and firm counter only work if the candidate is actually willing to decline
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Then name a specific total comp target and hold to it through the negotiation
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Adjust negotiation tactics accordingly; do not use the commitment close and do not make ultimatums you will not follow through on

industry_realities.common_belief: Glassdoor and PayScale give you accurate salary benchmarks for tech roles
industry_realities.actual_reality: These platforms wildly mishandle equity compensation and systematically underreport total comp for tech; levels.fyi and H-1B filings are the authoritative sources for tech compensation
industry_realities.implication: Candidates who benchmark using Glassdoor anchor their negotiations too low and leave significant money on the table; switch to levels.fyi before any negotiation

industry_realities.common_belief: Salary is the most important and negotiable component of an offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: Equity is often 30-60% of total comp at large tech companies; signing bonuses are one-time and the easiest component for companies to approve; base salary is often the most constrained by band limits
industry_realities.implication: When base is capped, shift negotiation focus to signing bonus and equity refresh rather than continuing to push on base

industry_realities.common_belief: Recruiters will volunteer all relevant compensation details when making an offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: Critical details like the option exercise window, fully diluted ownership percentage, liquidation preferences, and 409A valuations are almost never volunteered; they must be explicitly asked for
industry_realities.implication: Candidates must run a structured checklist of equity questions on every offer call or risk agreeing to terms that significantly reduce actual compensation value

industry_realities.common_belief: Asking for more will make you seem greedy and risk the offer being rescinded
industry_realities.actual_reality: People who negotiate get an average 18.83% more than those who accept the first offer; offers are almost never rescinded for polite, well-framed negotiation
industry_realities.implication: The expected value of negotiating is highly positive and the downside risk is near zero when done with warmth and professionalism

industry_realities.common_belief: A competing offer from a company you wouldn't actually take is just as useful as a real one
industry_realities.actual_reality: Fabricating or exaggerating competing offers is explicitly flagged as something to never do; the tactic only works when genuine and can destroy the offer if discovered
industry_realities.implication: Only cite competing offers that are real, documented, and from companies you would actually consider; the leverage comes from credibility, not the existence of a number

industry_realities.common_belief: The equity value the recruiter quotes is what the equity is worth
industry_realities.actual_reality: Recruiter-quoted equity valuations for pre-IPO companies are based on hoped-for future valuations, not current 409A values; early-stage equity should be valued at $0 for budgeting
industry_realities.implication: Apply stage-appropriate discount rates to all equity: public companies at 80-90% of market value, Series C-D at 60-90%, early-stage at $0 for planning purposes

industry_realities.common_belief: Signing bonuses are part of your ongoing compensation
industry_realities.actual_reality: Signing bonuses are one-time payments; your year-2 and beyond compensation is materially lower unless the bonus is renewed, which is not standard
industry_realities.implication: Always model annual recurring compensation without the signing bonus to understand the true ongoing offer value; signing bonus is a bridge, not a salary component

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares offers by base salary, gravitates toward whichever offer has the highest headline number
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Builds a full annual total comp model for each offer: annualized equity at stage-appropriate discount, target bonus, and base; excludes signing bonus from recurring calc
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Two offers at the same base can differ by $100,000+ annually; junior methodology produces wrong rankings and systematically undervalues public company offers

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts recruiter's equity valuation and treats it as real money, excited by the large headline number
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Asks for 409A valuation, liquidation preferences, fully diluted percentage, and exercise window; applies stage-appropriate discount before counting equity in financial planning
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Inflated equity expectations lead to accepting lower base salaries in exchange for equity that may never materialize

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Responds with immediate positivity or concern on the first call, either accepting verbally or revealing dissatisfaction
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Expresses genuine excitement, asks structured clarifying questions, requests time to consider, and schedules a follow-up call before any decision
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Responding on the first call forfeits leverage; the pause creates space for preparation and signals deliberateness that strengthens the negotiating position

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Frames compensation asks around personal need ('I need more because of my student loans') or vague dissatisfaction
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Frames asks around objective market data (levels.fyi benchmarks, competing offers) or company-first reasoning ('I want to make it easy to choose you')
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Personal need framing is easy for recruiters to dismiss; market data and competing offer framing creates external justification that is harder to counter and more credible

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats base salary as the only negotiable variable; gives up when told base is capped
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Knows the full priority order of negotiable dimensions (equity refresh, signing bonus, vacation, remote flexibility, accelerated vesting, professional development, equipment) and moves through them systematically
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Stopping at base salary leaves multiple high-value, easily approvable components untouched; companies expect candidates to ask across dimensions

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Relies on Glassdoor or PayScale for salary benchmarks; cites ranges without specifying level
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Uses levels.fyi for total comp by level and company, H-1B filings for authoritative base salary data, and Blind for peer compensation validation; always specifies the exact level when citing data
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Inaccurate benchmark data produces low anchors; level-specific data is required for the benchmark to be credible in negotiation

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Runs interview processes sequentially or randomly; gets caught with misaligned timelines; may fabricate offers out of desperation
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Deliberately sequences processes by company speed (large corps first, startups second) to engineer timeline convergence; only cites real offers; uses urgency scripts to accelerate lagging processes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Deliberate timing is what makes competing offers available simultaneously; without it, offers expire before alternatives are ready and leverage disappears
